headline: Vernacular school choices: the quality crisis nobody wants to name
context: Parents of all races are fleeing national schools — but the decades-old unity-vs-culture debate hides a governance failure, a Tamil school collapse, and a growing class divide in education.

cards.big: The vernacular school fight is stuck on unity vs culture — the courts settled the law, but parents keep leaving
cards.sub: The Federal Court refused leave to appeal in February 2024, preserving constitutionality. Yet the real drivers of school choice have little to do with heritage.

cards.big: 69% of middle-class Malay parents preferred SJKC for academic quality — but no data proves SJKC is actually better
cards.sub: An ISEAS survey of 928 Malay respondents found quality, discipline and location drove the choice. SJKC and SK share the same syllabus — the gap is perceived, not proven.

cards.big: Tamil schools are collapsing: 37 have 10 or fewer students, and 67% sit in rural areas the Indian community has left behind
cards.sub: SJKT enrolment fell from over 100,000 in 2011 to 72,080 by 2025. Federal SJKT allocation was raised back to RM50M in early 2026. No Tamil secondary schools exist.

cards.big: Malaysian enrolment in international schools rose 34% in five years — class is reshaping education faster than ethnicity
cards.sub: MOE: 88,951 citizens in international schools by 2024. The deeper class divide is the RM7B tuition industry that stratifies outcomes within public schools.

cards.big: The real question: what specifically is driving parents away — governance rigidity, curriculum imbalance, or discipline failures?
Stop debating whether vernacular schools should exist — start asking why national schools keep losing the families they serve
